---
title: "Frequency of Refilling My Water Bottle"
description: "A coding project I completed for ENV S 193DS: Statistics for Environmental Science"
date: 06-04-2026
categories: [RStudio, Data Visualization, Class Project]
image: blue-bottle.jpg
draft: false
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

A relationship question I wanted to look into was how does temperature influence how often I refill my water bottle?  However, I knew there was going to be other variables involved that may affect the data for temperature and the frequency I refilled.  Therefore, every day from 4/26 to 6/4, for every time I refilled my water bottle, I would record the temperature, the amount of classes I had up until then, the weather, how many times I ate up until then, and if I had exercised or not.

---

To organize my data, I cleaned up the column names for my explanatory variables and capitalized the label names from the weather column.  

```{r}

#read in packages
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(janitor)
#stored personal data as an object called personal
personal <- read_csv(here("data", "personal.csv"))

#created new object from personal
personal_clean <- personal |>
#cleaned column names    
  clean_names() |> 
#mutated the existing weather column  
  mutate(weather = case_when(
#filled in Sunny to replace sunny    
    weather == "sunny"  ~ "Sunny",
#filled in Cloudy to replace cloudy    
    weather == "cloudy" ~ "Cloudy",
#filled in Windy to replace windy    
    weather == "windy"  ~ "Windy",
#filled in Night to replace night    
    weather == "night"  ~ "Night"))

```

---

Before making a plot between temperature and the frequency of refilling my water bottle, I wanted to make a plot that evaluated the data of another explanatory variable.  In this case, I picked out weather and created a bar chart, so it would compare the frequencies among my four weather labels that occurred during the whole duration of my data collection: cloudy, night, sunny, and windy.  Looking at the bar chart, I refill my water bottle the most times when it is sunny, which makes sense since the weather in Santa Barbara is immaculate more often than not.   

```{r}

#base layer: ggplot with x-axis
ggplot(data = personal_clean, 
#x-axis is weather and y-axis is frequency of refilling water bottle
       mapping = aes(x = weather)) +
#changed bar colors for all weather types   
  geom_bar(fill = "navyblue") +
#changed the label for x-axis: Weather 
  labs(x = "Weather",
#changed the label for y-axis: Frequency of Refilling Water Bottle        
       y = "Frequency of Refilling Water Bottle",
#added a title to the bar chart        
       title = "Frequency of Refilling Water Bottle Based on Weather") +
#used a different theme from default  
  theme_minimal() +
#removed panel background  
  theme(panel.background = element_blank()) + 
#removed major and minor gridlines   
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) 

```

---

The best way to plot the data between temperature and the frequency I refill my water bottle was through a scatterplot since I am evaluating whether or not exactly two variables are related to each other.  The x-axis would be the temperature (°F) and the y-axis would be the frequency that I refilled my water bottle.  Unfortunately though, when I plotted all of these points, many of them were basically scattered throughout the whole figure, so there did not seem to a correlation between both variables.  

```{r}

#base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = personal_clean, 
#x-axis: temperature (Fahrenheit)
       mapping = aes(x = temperature_f)) +
#develops a scatterplot for frequency response variables, which can have colors
  geom_point(stat = "count", color = "darkred", size = 1) + 
#relabel x- and y-axes, added a title
  labs(x = "Temperature (°F)",
       y = "Frequency of Refilling Water Bottle",
       title = "Frequency of Refilling Water Bottle Depending on Temperature") + 
#changed theme from default  
  theme_minimal()+
#removed panel background  
  theme(panel.background = element_blank()) + 
#removed major and minor gridlines   
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()) 

```
